The UN Food and Agriculture Organization had estimated that figure in one of its annual reports; they even made a 50-foot banner reading "One Billion Hungry" and hung it on their headquarters in Rome. It is indeed not a glorious thing that up to one billion people in the world are hungry, but accurate data indicate that "one billion" is not a fact. This week, in the 2012 State of Food Security report, the FAO quietly revised the number of hungry down to 868 million, bringing it back from the spike in 2008–10. The table above shows the latest set of estimates (left) compared with 2010 (right). The specific comparison is somewhat complicated because some of the lines in the two charts do not represent the same periods, but the overall change is clear: tracking world food prices shows that although there were large price fluctuations in 2008–10, the number of hungry people worldwide remained at a relatively stable level in the first decade of the 21st century.
